text: Copper’s ability to activate and catalyze the CO2 reduction reaction (RR) remains one of the greatest mysteries in the field of electrocatalysis. While the effect of surface roughening due to atomic vacancies and adatoms is widely accepted, the exact structure of the active sites remains unclear. Herein, we elucidate the Cu surface structure as a function of potential and varying CO and H coverage through the integration of density functional theory (DFT) and global optimization methods to form a potential-dependent grand canonical ensemble. While the metastable mixed CO/H coverage regime, which has the highest Cu surface atom concentration, is unfavorable, it is unavoidable during the operation of the electrode. A quasi-kinetic Monte Carlo model is used to elucidate the dynamic behavior of the surface during a cathodic scan. The metastable Cu*CO structures are identified along the reaction pathway. Their bonding and mechanism of formation are elucidated by an in-depth analysis of the chemical bonding in these structures. Molecular dynamics and free energy calculations are employed to elucidate the diffusion and dimerization pathways of the Cu*CO structures. This study not only elucidates the Cu surface structure under electrochemical conditions but also introduces a methodology to model the nonequilibrium behavior of the surface restructuring under mixed adsorbate conditions.
